--- title: "BairesDev 90-day assessment and ask" id: 14435 created: 2025-04-12T16:32:22Z modified: 2025-04-16T02:43:27Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "0f970322-2ead-4731-9d87-1984124d6e34" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.link" - id: "f8775df9-3639-4b3b-a555-186990f12a25" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: - url: "https://www.cvedetails.com/vulnerability-list/vendor_id-26/product_id-251/version_id-412462/microsoft-sql-server-2005.html" title: "https://www.cvedetails.com/vulnerability-list/vendor_id-26/product_id-251/version_id-412462/Microsoft-Sql-Server-2005.html" source: notes://showNote?identifier=9dc54b52-d372-47e8-9c89-696008e6f707 --- BairesDev 90-day assessment and ask NO CRM is killing our dismal sales funnel * Swarm and burn is our primary approach to prospects Reach targets highly quantified ICPs but overlooks two even more important attributes from a sales funnel perspective * Intent: Google Search won because it served ads to users with high intent (never mind they are now in the prison of 10 blue links) * Propensity: Pain is not measured by Reach—nor easily measured—but “spray and prey” has severely attenuated limits The feedback loop is broken * We are GREAT at collaboration and HORRIBLE at information sharing * According to the coconut telegraph, Frank has been working on KPMG and PE/VC playbook—when can that be shared? Are we open to a “15 day wind-down on exit” for PE/VC? Our distaste/disregard for GTM partnerships severely limits our distribution opportunities (i.e., Reach) * Bain is a great embedded GTM example: Eleni makes money while she sleeps (good on her!) Staff augmentation is necessary for now but not sufficient as an offering * Managed services Our in-house platform choices are utterly embarrassing * We use Databricks for our OLTP pseudo-CRM apps (all the portals, but especially Reach) We use SQL Server 2005 in AWS for our company data backbone * [https://www.cvedetails.com/vulnerability-list/vendor_id-26/product_id-251/version_id-412462/Microsoft-Sql-Server-2005.html](https://www.cvedetails.com/vulnerability-list/vendor_id-26/product_id-251/version_id-412462/Microsoft-Sql-Server-2005.html) Our approach has NO ACCOUNT OR SEGMENT OR VERTICAL STRATEGY, only “spray and prey” tactics triggered by HIGHLY QUANTIFIED ICP SCORES [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=9dc54b52-d372-47e8-9c89-696008e6f707)

content: --- title: "Beyond Software - BairesDev Press Releases | Latest News and Updates" id: 13503 created: 2024-12-17T03:36:00Z modified: 2024-12-28T21:36:14Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "34059719-c1a8-4dc1-8c05-1001e91c1866" type: "public.jpeg" content: "34059719-c1a8-4dc1-8c05-1001e91c1866.jpg" - id: "ab0dcf74-ac37-4df8-acc3-05d788ee01ec" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: - url: "https://assets.bairesdev.com/pr/forbes-argentina-the-incredible-story-of-bairesdev_mrdhhs" title: "here" - url: "https://www.forbesargentina.com/innovacion/nacho-marco-increible-historia-bairesdev-unico-unicornio-tech-capital-privado-argentino-impacto-global-n49605" title: "here" - url: "https://press.bairesdev.com/beyond-software/" title: "Beyond Software - BairesDev Press Releases | Latest News and Updates" source: notes://showNote?identifier=a077a495-ef87-4e6e-9f44-f6910e9548fd --- **Beyond Software - BairesDev Press Releases | Latest News and Updates** By **Pablo Wahnon**, Editor at Forbes Argentina In 2009, Ignacio “Nacho” De Marco found himself no longer wandering the streets of Buenos Aires but navigating the vibrant landscapes of Australia. Uprooted by Argentina’s tumultuous climate at just 25, he embarked on an unexpected journey of creating the world’s largest bootstrapped software outsourcing company. Nobody would have guessed he’d be there. Nacho went to a public high school, “one of those with a number, not a famous one,” he clarifies to Forbes Argentina in an exclusive interview in Los Gatos, south of Silicon Valley. His passion was for accounting: “I thought I was going to be an accountant; I loved tax issues.” But his father, a software specialist, encouraged him to help with some projects, and he discovered a new world: “He paid me $1 an hour, and it felt like touching the sky.” After studying Computer Science at university, his specialization opened the door to a stable position in Australia as a Technical Project Manager, where he rubbed elbows and managed various projects. The security of a predictable career did not extinguish the entrepreneurial flame within him: “Together with Paul Azorin, who was still in Buenos Aires, we started to get clients. Each of us had our regular jobs, but we felt that our idea could materialize into a company, which already had a name: BairesDev.” He and Azorin had met in a specialization course in Management of Technological Projects and had the vision of a promising business that could scale from Latin America: software outsourcing. There was a significant arbitrage opportunity, and they decided to focus on the United States. “Our competitors had the talent, but they struggled to reach beyond the local market or Latin America. So, we decided to work only with U.S. clients, but it wasn’t easy at first,” he says. During those early days, “we earned much more from our stable jobs. But our hearts beat when we worked on our venture. I would return from the office, and at night, I’d put on the BairesDev hat. In that way, Buenos Aires was always close,” he reminisces. Working abroad was a good idea but hard to execute without international contacts. Added to this was the fact they had no investors (nor did they try to find any). “We were convinced we needed to be working for clients in the United States. We had the best time zone, and the level of English in LATAM was becoming increasingly proficient. The cultural component is quite close as well. The talent from our region adds value. We are here to make concrete contributions, and that is something our clients appreciate. They value that we care about doing things right, not just appearing to do so, and that’s why we have been able to establish lasting relationships,” shares De Marco. In the vast expanse of the tech industry, where giants loom large, BairesDev charted its own successful course. The contrast between what a programmer earns in places like San Francisco versus Argentina was clear, but leveraging this was not new. Globant, the Argentine software company with a market cap of about $10 billion, had already taken advantage of this. BairesDev’s innovation was not the business model of software outsourcing, but discovering a new way to take flight within a territory already traversed by others. If the rest were traveling by land, they planned to create technology to fly. At the time when the airplane was about to be invented, several teams were competing. The group led by Samuel Pierpont Langley was adored by the media. He came from Harvard and the Smithsonian, and had fame and contacts. However, the airplane was invented by the unknown Wright brothers. They had no money. But they did have a dream that went far beyond appearing in a photo. That passion and their experience in manufacturing bicycles led them to discover the crucial component for an airplane to fly. The key was creating the wind tunnel, a component that allowed testing different types of airplanes until finding the most suitable one. De Marco and Azorín came from software and thought they could change the traditional way of selling it. Instead of hiring salespeople, they proposed creating specialized digital marketing software to help them. “If we sell software development to other companies, we have to also be able to do something disruptive for our own,” they thought. Thus, their great invention, their wind tunnel, was creating a digital hub that connected the two ends: market needs with the capabilities of programmers. They thought of the organization as a platform for a “two-sided market,” where the aim is not only to be attractive to clients but also to suppliers; in this case, the coders. **GROWTH** Placing the marketplace at the center stage was a disruptive move that happened as they became familiar with the industry. The first years were much less structured, and the real challenge was securing their first U.S. client. They had no contacts or marketing resources, so they began a systematic process of prospecting through LinkedIn. Diving through so much data allowed them to understand the challenges they would face when eventually creating their proprietary cold contact technology. The first company to hire them came from the gaming industry. “They had a Brazilian CEO, who understood he could work better with us compared to companies from India. Moreover, they had already had poor experiences with outsourcing to India. He valued the component of cultural proximity. When he saw we were good, he recommended us to another client, this time in the finance industry,” remembers De Marco. And he adds: “When we had five clients, we entered a new stage, where we looked to scale.” Meanwhile, their passion for BairesDev competed against their steady jobs. “I remember telling my wife: ‘BairesDev is going to work because I earn a third of what I do in my day job.’” Returning to Argentina and diving fully into BairesDev was “taking a potentially stupid risk because it’s not like we had many clients, and that makes you vulnerable to any unforeseen event. But I felt it was the path I had to take. We went all in with Paul. Initially, all we were looking for was to reach a point where we felt some stability,” recalls De Marco. They did not have investors to support them if things went wrong; they had to maintain and grow with their own resources. This phase lasted about five years, where the founders did everything from marketing to collections. As they grew, they started adding people beyond the coders. By 2014, 400 people were working at BairesDev. “I made the decision with my wife to go to San Francisco. We saw that BairesDev was now stable. We had a variety of clients, ensuring we wouldn’t go bankrupt in two years. We moved from a survival mindset, typical of people in Argentina, to seeing that we could be a giant company. We thought we could be among the 20 largest companies in the world in our market. With our changed mindset we did a lot of work on brand improvement and marketing. We started to invest fearlessly. And we decided that the headquarters would be in San Francisco,” details De Marco. Growth was leveraged by their own marketing tools, which allowed them to reach new clients in an automated way. “The number of salespeople we have, compared to our competition, is very low. Our model combines a few salespeople with a lot of specialized software pertaining to ads, content, and digital marketing in a much deeper way than what exists in the market, even today. Our advancement is so significant that it’s not easy to match us when it comes to quality lead generation,” he highlights. This digital wind tunnel allowed them to continue developing new marketing models to further drive their growth without external investment. “In hindsight, I’m glad it was this way. You get to dedicate yourself completely to your clients and employees without having to worry about anyone else. Often the pressure from investors influences decisions to be made more on how the market will react rather than what the company needs in the medium and long term. For example, the market expects you to lay off people during a recession. But perhaps without that pressure, you would arrange things differently, to not lose your intangible value, which is your talent,” explains De Marco. The BairesDev model grew at great speed, incorporating clients of various sectors and sizes: from startups to corporations like Coca-Cola, Adobe, eBay, and HP, among others. They had entered a new phase of the company, and, while BairesDev remained a private company, they brought on partners when they encountered great talent. Today, De Marco owns slightly less than half of the company, with Azorin and Fernando Galano as the other major partners. They also added Pablo Chamorro and Facundo Molina, who added their expertise to the marketing platform. **THE EXPLOSION** While the machinery of BairesDev continued to grow, something unforeseen happened: Covid emerged with unusual force, and businesses suffered. “We had two stages. At first, I was very worried that certain clients were falling through, like those in tourism, and some planned projects were getting canceled. It felt like everything could crumble. Fortunately, that feeling only lasted two or three months. We did not make any hasty decisions and kept the company steady. In the second stage, the opposite happened: although the market was struggling globally, in 2020 we grew by 56% and in 2021 by 156%, totally unexpected. We’re talking about a level of growth that’s very difficult to keep up with,” De Marco shares. How did this happen? BairesDev, along with other companies with a similar model, were prepared for the pandemic. “One of the reasons we didn’t win some business earlier was because clients wanted the programmers to be in their offices. We lost out in those situations. Our advantage was that our talent was mainly in Argentina, which made conversations with potential clients difficult. It wasn’t enough to show them our proposal that guaranteed they would have the most talented people at a lower price,” recalls De Marco. “The pandemic validated the business model we had conceived 10 years prior. We were better prepared than anyone in that period because we were already operating remotely. Maybe the last major barrier to competing globally was overcome,” boasts De Marco. The explosive growth of BairesDev transformed it into an important player in software development. Now the company has more than 4,000 employees. Talent from different countries began to be added to the pool of Argentine coders. Today their engineers come from 50 different countries, 90% being from Latin America. BairesDev’s digital hub allows them to find the best developers thanks to a highly effective talent acquisition pipeline. Amazingly, in 2021 they received more than 1.5 million applications. The talent selection system, backed by artificial intelligence, enables them to hire the most skilled technical profiles. This figure pales in comparison to what happened in 2022, when they received 2.4 million engineers, eager to be a part of BairesDev. “When you reach large numbers, you have the possibility to filter the best of the best, and that’s an important quality seal that our clients appreciate,” he highlights. “From the analysis of these applications, we identified several trends in tech talent from the region. For example, between 2015 and 2021, the number of women applying to the industry increased by 400%. The female professionals who applied to technical positions showed greater interest in web, mobile, and testing,” reveals Andrea Modarelli, Head of PR at BairesDev. The firm reported a revenue of $338 million in 2022, marking a 71% growth from the $198 million recorded the previous year. Although the figures for 2023 are pending, projections suggest it will exceed $350 million, positioning BairesDev as one of the tech sector’s rapidly evolving bootstrapped unicorns. Over the past four years, the company has seen an average annual growth rate of 86%. With a diverse clientele spanning over 100 industry sectors and 27 countries, BairesDev’s largest client alone leverages 230+ BairesDev developers. The work carried out has led them to have experience in more than 142 technologies. As for AI-related projects, they have 81 projects in more than 20 industries. How much is the company worth? “It’s not for sale,” assures De Marco. When Globant had the same number of employees, it billed $220 million versus $330 million for the company created by De Marco and Azorin. This means their business model is optimized by around 50% compared to the market leader. Market estimates suggest BairesDev could be worth at least $2 billion USD, crowning it as a new unicorn created by Argentinian talent. Reflecting on their journey, De Marco shares, “Two years back, we realized our business model thrived independently of external funding. This revelation spurred us to explore how our strategies could benefit other firms. Having developed our marketing software, sales, and recruitment frameworks, we extended our model to a select few partners. The results were astonishing, with a threefold increase in their sales within 14 months.” This success led to the inception of BDev Ventures, aimed at propelling the growth of companies within their ecosystem. “We start with small companies and put them through this improvement machine. We are currently engaging with 60 companies, across many different sectors like HR, Finance and Healthcare. The model is similar because they need to sell to and recruit people. We work with them for free for 3 or 4 months, so our investor risk decreases,” says De Marco. “I will never be an expert in investments, but I can guarantee that if we significantly improve their billing and recruitment, and give them money that enables them to operate better, the chances of them doing well are very high. That’s what happened to us,” concludes De Marco. A journey that began with just a dollar an hour, wound up creating a business with a multibillion dollar valuation. After charting his own path, De Marco’s new adventure is creating roads for others so they can chase dreams of their own. Read the PDF Version [here](https://assets.bairesdev.com/pr/forbes-argentina-the-incredible-story-of-bairesdev_mrdhhs). **This translation has been authorized by the writer. The original text in Spanish can be found *[*here*](https://www.forbesargentina.com/innovacion/nacho-marco-increible-historia-bairesdev-unico-unicornio-tech-capital-privado-argentino-impacto-global-n49605)*.* [Beyond Software - BairesDev Press Releases | Latest News and Updates](https://press.bairesdev.com/beyond-software/) [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=a077a495-ef87-4e6e-9f44-f6910e9548fd)

--- title: "Frank Rodorigo ChatGPT prompts" id: 15067 created: 2025-06-05T22:24:57Z modified: 2025-06-05T22:24:58Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "839a7e66-0783-4eec-8342-9d071fe54f1e" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=8c7144e4-b2f1-4ea2-8677-acb2b68be2df --- Frank Rodorigo ChatGPT prompts @Michael Shaler I general create a prompt from past prompts and the ask ChatGPT to refine it based on the specifics (in this case Brian's Request). Here is the refined prompt that ChatGPT created to produce the document: DEEP-RESEARCH PROMPT — BAIRESDEV MAGENTO / SHOPWARE → SHOPIFY EXPERTISE BACKGROUND A colleague asked whether BairesDev has proven expertise with Magento and Shopware e-commerce platforms—particularly in migrating merchants from Magento or Shopware to Shopify / Shopify Plus. One of our referral partners may have a large pipeline that depends on this capability. RESEARCH OBJECTIVES Confirm platform experience * Document BairesDev’s track record in building, customizing, and supporting Magento and Shopware stores. Assess migration credentials * Highlight successful projects where BairesDev migrated clients from Magento / Shopware to Shopify, noting methodologies, tools, and risk-mitigation practices. Surface case studies & metrics * Capture for each engagement: client / industry, scope, tech stack, measurable outcomes (conversion lift, page-load improvement, cost savings), quotes or testimonials. Pinpoint differentiators * Summarize unique assets (LATAM senior talent, accelerators, reusable migration frameworks, 24×7 support, security certifications, etc.). Compile source references * Provide filenames or page titles for every data point (BairesDev.com, uploaded decks, press releases, Clutch reviews, LinkedIn articles, etc.). SOURCE MATERIAL * BairesDev website: Services → E-commerce, Magento, Shopify, Case Studies, Blog * Uploaded internal files: capability decks, success-story PDFs/TXT, technical white papers * Third-party evidence: news coverage, platform partner pages, analyst reviews, Clutch, G2, LinkedIn posts * Additional context from another Principal: • Shopify projects (P&G Native, Global Shapewear, Urban Outfitters) showing GA4 migration, custom bundle builder, fraud control, 40 K orders/hour, 38 % profit growth, etc. • Migration parallels: monolith → micro-services, React/Vue/Liquid front-end rebuilds, Salesforce & ERP integrations, high-throughput multi-country ops. DELIVERABLES * Executive Summary (1–2 pages) — bullet-point overview of capabilities, proof points, and key differentiators. * Case-Study Appendix — one concise paragraph per project, including metrics and source reference. * Bibliography — flat list of all URLs or document names for verification. FORMAT & TONE * Concise, professional, client-ready — suitable for pasting into a proposal or briefing deck. * Use headings and bullets for rapid scanning. * Cite every claim immediately (document name or page title only; no live hyperlinks). OUTPUT Return the deliverables as plain text in a Google-Doc-ready outline (no hyperlinks). [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=8c7144e4-b2f1-4ea2-8677-acb2b68be2df)

--- title: "MSFT SQL Server 2005 SP2 Security Vulnerabilities and CVEs" id: 14459 created: 2025-04-16T01:09:27Z modified: 2025-04-16T01:13:18Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "584a68d9-2394-4402-81f7-d5ca0ad5ba20" type: "com.apple.notes.table" content: "\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n
CVEPublishedLast UpdateMax CVSS Base ScoreEPSS ScoreCISA KEV AddedPublic Exploit ExistsSummary
CVE-2009-31262009-10-142025-04-099.366.23%Integer overflow in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System SP1 and SP2, Office Project 2002 SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, Office Word Viewer, Word Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer, Office PowerPoint Viewer 2007 Gold, SP1, and SP2, Office Compatibility Pack for Word, Excel, and PowerPoint 2007 File Formats SP1 and SP2, Expression Web, Expression Web 2, Groove 2007 Gold and SP1, Works 8.5, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2 and SP3, Report Viewer 2005 SP1, Report Viewer 2008 Gold and SP1, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a crafted PNG image file, aka \"GDI+ PNG Integer Overflow Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2009-25282009-10-142025-04-099.356.15%GDI+ in Microsoft Office XP SP3 does not properly handle malformed objects in Office Art Property Tables, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a crafted Office document that triggers memory corruption, aka \"Memory Corruption Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2009-25042009-10-142025-04-099.358.76%Multiple integer overflows in unspecified APIs in GDI+ in Microsoft .NET Framework 1.1 SP1, .NET Framework 2.0 SP1 and SP2, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Windows Server 2003 SP2, Vista Gold and SP1, Server 2008 Gold, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System SP1 and SP2, Office Project 2002 SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, Office Word Viewer, Word Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer, Office PowerPoint Viewer 2007 Gold, SP1, and SP2, Office Compatibility Pack for Word, Excel, and PowerPoint 2007 File Formats SP1 and SP2, Expression Web, Expression Web 2, Groove 2007 Gold and SP1, Works 8.5, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2 and SP3, Report Viewer 2005 SP1, Report Viewer 2008 Gold and SP1, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allow remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via (1) a crafted XAML browser application (XBAP), (2) a crafted ASP.NET application, or (3) a crafted .NET Framework application, aka \"GDI+ .NET API Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2009-25032009-10-142025-04-099.354.17%GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Windows Server 2003 SP2, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System SP1 and SP2, Office Project 2002 SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, Office Word Viewer, Word Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer, Office PowerPoint Viewer 2007 Gold, SP1, and SP2, Office Compatibility Pack for Word, Excel, and PowerPoint 2007 File Formats SP1 and SP2, Expression Web, Expression Web 2, Groove 2007 Gold and SP1, Works 8.5, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2 and SP3, Report Viewer 2005 SP1, Report Viewer 2008 Gold and SP1, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 does not properly allocate an unspecified buffer, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a crafted TIFF image file that triggers memory corruption, aka \"GDI+ TIFF Memory Corruption Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2009-25022009-10-142025-04-099.348.99%Buffer overflow in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System SP1 and SP2, Office Project 2002 SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, Office Word Viewer, Word Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer, Office PowerPoint Viewer 2007 Gold, SP1, and SP2, Office Compatibility Pack for Word, Excel, and PowerPoint 2007 File Formats SP1 and SP2, Expression Web, Expression Web 2, Groove 2007 Gold and SP1, Works 8.5, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2 and SP3, Report Viewer 2005 SP1, Report Viewer 2008 Gold and SP1, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a crafted TIFF image file, aka \"GDI+ TIFF Buffer Overflow Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2009-25012009-10-142025-04-099.361.37%Heap-based buffer overflow in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System SP1 and SP2, Office Project 2002 SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, Office Word Viewer, Word Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer, Office PowerPoint Viewer 2007 Gold, SP1, and SP2, Office Compatibility Pack for Word, Excel, and PowerPoint 2007 File Formats SP1 and SP2, Expression Web, Expression Web 2, Groove 2007 Gold and SP1, Works 8.5, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2 and SP3, Report Viewer 2005 SP1, Report Viewer 2008 Gold and SP1, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a crafted PNG image file, aka \"GDI+ PNG Heap Overflow Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2009-25002009-10-142025-04-099.370.75%Integer overflow in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System SP1 and SP2, Office Project 2002 SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, Office Word Viewer, Word Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer 2003 Gold and SP3, Office Excel Viewer, Office PowerPoint Viewer 2007 Gold, SP1, and SP2, Office Compatibility Pack for Word, Excel, and PowerPoint 2007 File Formats SP1 and SP2, Expression Web, Expression Web 2, Groove 2007 Gold and SP1, Works 8.5, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2 and SP3, Report Viewer 2005 SP1, Report Viewer 2008 Gold and SP1, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a crafted WMF image file, aka \"GDI+ WMF Integer Overflow Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-54162008-12-102025-04-099.089.07%PublicHeap-based buffer overflow in Microsoft SQL Server 2000 SP4, 8.00.2050, 8.00.2039, and earlier; SQL Server 2000 Desktop Engine (MSDE 2000) SP4; SQL Server 2005 SP2 and 9.00.1399.06; SQL Server 2000 Desktop Engine (WMSDE) on Windows Server 2003 SP1 and SP2; and Windows Internal Database (WYukon) SP2 allows remote authenticated users to cause a denial of service (access violation exception) or execute arbitrary code by calling the sp_replwritetovarbin extended stored procedure with a set of invalid parameters that trigger memory overwrite, aka \"SQL Server sp_replwritetovarbin Limited Memory Overwrite Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-30152008-09-112025-04-099.371.54%Integer overflow in gdiplus.dll in GDI+ in Microsoft Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP2 and SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System Gold and SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, PowerPoint Viewer 2003, Works 8, Digital Image Suite 2006, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2, Report Viewer 2005 SP1 and 2008, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a BMP image file with a malformed BitMapInfoHeader that triggers a buffer overflow, aka \"GDI+ BMP Integer Overflow Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-30142008-09-112025-04-099.371.55%Buffer overflow in gdiplus.dll in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Server 2003 SP1 and SP2, Vista Gold and SP1, Server 2008, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP2 and SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System Gold and SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, PowerPoint Viewer 2003, Works 8, Digital Image Suite 2006, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2, Report Viewer 2005 SP1 and 2008, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a malformed WMF image file that triggers improper memory allocation, aka \"GDI+ WMF Buffer Overrun Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-30132008-09-112025-04-099.374.61%gdiplus.dll in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Server 2003 SP1 and SP2, Vista Gold and SP1, Server 2008, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP2 and SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System Gold and SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, PowerPoint Viewer 2003, Works 8, Digital Image Suite 2006, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2, Report Viewer 2005 SP1 and 2008, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a malformed GIF image file containing many extension markers for graphic control extensions and subsequent unknown labels, aka \"GDI+ GIF Parsing Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-30122008-09-112025-04-099.370.69%gdiplus.dll in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Server 2003 SP1 and SP2, Vista Gold and SP1, Server 2008, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP2 and SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System Gold and SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, PowerPoint Viewer 2003, Works 8, Digital Image Suite 2006, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2, Report Viewer 2005 SP1 and 2008, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 does not properly perform memory allocation, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a malformed EMF image file, aka \"GDI+ EMF Memory Corruption Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-01072008-07-082025-04-099.057.27%Integer underflow in SQL Server 7.0 SP4, 2000 SP4, 2005 SP1 and SP2, 2000 Desktop Engine (MSDE 2000) SP4, 2005 Express Edition SP1 and SP2, and 2000 Desktop Engine (WMSDE); Microsoft Data Engine (MSDE) 1.0 SP4; and Internal Database (WYukon) SP2 allows remote authenticated users to execute arbitrary code via a (1) SMB or (2) WebDAV pathname for an on-disk file (aka stored backup file) with a crafted record size value, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow, aka \"SQL Server Memory Corruption Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2008-01062008-07-082025-04-099.072.63%Buffer overflow in Microsoft SQL Server 2005 SP1 and SP2, and 2005 Express Edition SP1 and SP2, allows remote authenticated users to execute arbitrary code via a crafted insert statement.
CVE-2008-00862008-07-082025-04-099.073.36%Buffer overflow in the convert function in Microsoft SQL Server 2000 SP4, 2000 Desktop Engine (MSDE 2000) SP4, and 2000 Desktop Engine (WMSDE) allows remote authenticated users to execute arbitrary code via a crafted SQL expression.
CVE-2008-00852008-07-082025-04-095.027.54%SQL Server 7.0 SP4, 2000 SP4, 2005 SP1 and SP2, 2000 Desktop Engine (MSDE 2000) SP4, 2005 Express Edition SP1 and SP2, and 2000 Desktop Engine (WMSDE); Microsoft Data Engine (MSDE) 1.0 SP4; and Internal Database (WYukon) SP2 does not initialize memory pages when reallocating memory, which allows database operators to obtain sensitive information (database contents) via unknown vectors related to memory page reuse.
CVE-2007-53482008-09-112025-04-099.376.42%Integer overflow in GDI+ in Microsoft Internet Explorer 6 SP1, Windows XP SP2 and SP3, Server 2003 SP1 and SP2, Vista Gold and SP1, Server 2008, Office XP SP3, Office 2003 SP2 and SP3, 2007 Microsoft Office System Gold and SP1, Visio 2002 SP2, PowerPoint Viewer 2003, Works 8, Digital Image Suite 2006, SQL Server 2000 Reporting Services SP2, SQL Server 2005 SP2, Report Viewer 2005 SP1 and 2008, and Forefront Client Security 1.0 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via an image file with crafted gradient sizes in gradient fill input, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow related to GdiPlus.dll and VGX.DLL, aka \"GDI+ VML Buffer Overrun Vulnerability.\"
CVE-2007-48142007-09-112025-04-097.545.30%PotentialBuffer overflow in the SQLServer ActiveX control in the Distributed Management Objects OLE DLL (sqldmo.dll) 2000.085.2004.00 in Microsoft SQL Server Enterprise Manager 8.05.2004 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a long second argument to the Start method.

--- title: "Note to Luisa" id: 14326 created: 2025-03-18T14:21:51Z modified: 2025-03-19T02:35:46Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "29be92f4-b755-4acf-9f12-b4fa61490d41" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: - url: "https://support.google.com/mail/answer/12921167?sjid=2142794404128797994-nc" title: "https://support.google.com/mail/answer/12921167?sjid=2142794404128797994-NC" source: notes://showNote?identifier=1d64861e-ccc2-49fd-b5a6-21208377e214 --- Note to Luisa Luisa, I reached out to Kath first (she’s always so helpful) and then Mirella (also so helpful) who encouraged me to reach out to you. I want to work more effectively and MUCH more efficiently in Reach than my current approach seems to enable—and while this may be a combination of user error and need for more training, I certainly would hope we could work together toward much better collaborative optimization of outcomes. I fully appreciate the dilemma the Reach addresses is pan vs. zoom, alternatively depth vs. breadth: how do we uncover all the buyers hidden in our breadth networks but still engage in deep, authentic, and meaningful conversations with our highest propensity buyers in order to accelerate sales engagements? I have seen dozens if not hundreds of automated emails go out under my signature and it’s no surprise to say that few actually sound like me—and I’ve gotten blunt private feedback from friends: “Dude, tell your marketing team to stop sending me AI slop—or at least make it sound like YOU.” I understand the Reach team works hard to improve these processes, but I would like to ask for the ability to prioritize and automate both my messaging and tracking much better than current workflows enable. My specific request is that I can receive a CSV download of all of my reach tasks (Task, First Name, Last Name, company, email, phone, LinkedIn URL) so that I can do the following operations offline: **Ensure data quality is improved and preserved**. In many cases, my senior executive prospects have multiple roles at multiple companies and the company name we capture in Reach is often not the target—and email addresses are often as a result incorrect. AI can’t easily solve this challenge unless we algorithmically apply much higher precision entity resolution, and that is an expensive challenge (one of my opportunities is to provide staff augmentation services for a company that builds very pricey ER software solutions). **Prioritize contacts and sequencing correctly**. An easy example here: I have the good fortune to have a good relationship with John Thompson, former CEO at Symantec. Reach asked me to text John (I declined), but I want to circle back with John in his current role (chairman of the board at Microsoft) if/when Nacho is ready to meet with the executive leadership at Microsoft—and, quite understandably, not until then. Hard to have this conversation with Reach on current terms. **Automate outreach and tracking**: Gmail supports sending 1,000 emails a day leveraging Google Sheets for mail merge: [https://support.google.com/mail/answer/12921167?sjid=2142794404128797994-NC](https://support.google.com/mail/answer/12921167?sjid=2142794404128797994-NC). I want to leverage this capability to better personalize and automate my email engagement, including tracking non-delivery and opt-outs and most importantly, my follow-ups to any responses I get. Reach is a great starting point from the marketing perspective, but it buts considerable cognitive and time-and-motion burden on my Reach workflows. I want to find a better way here and am asking for your support—your team sends automated emails under my signature, I am simply asking for similar capabilities on my Reach tasks. **Lastly, automated Reach updates**: Given the volume-focused metrics associated with Reach tasks, I also want to be able to automate status updates within Reach to ensure both your team and I get credit for our efforts and generate further collaborative opportunities to optimize our Reach engagements even better. Again, I want to find a better way here and am asking for your support to make me more efficient and effective. Thanks, Michael [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=1d64861e-ccc2-49fd-b5a6-21208377e214)

--- title: "Prompt Chain for BD" id: 14987 created: 2025-05-26T21:09:53Z modified: 2025-05-26T21:42:45Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "b52730d1-7ce3-444b-b53d-7424a3d48dc9" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=297c3c2e-78d3-4f0e-bb9f-e7703469ccc1 --- Prompt Chain for BD [COMPANY NAME]=BairesDev [INDUSTRY]=IT Services [UNIQUE ASSETS]=Skilled software developers in the same time zone as customers in North America Step 1: Context and Objectives Identify the primary objective for uncovering the company's unique value. List what you believe sets [COMPANY NAME] apart from its competitors in [INDUSTRY]. Provide an overview of the current market context and the challenges the company faces. ~Step 2: Strengths and Differentiators Analysis Detail the unique assets and strengths of [COMPANY NAME]. Consider the following: * What products or services offer distinct features? * Are there proprietary technologies, exclusive partnerships, or unique processes? * How does the company’s approach differ from its competitors? List these points as a bullet list. ~Step 3: Gathering Evidence and Market Position Collect supporting evidence that reinforces the uniqueness of [COMPANY NAME]. Include: * Customer testimonials or case studies * Comparative analysis with key competitors in [INDUSTRY] * Industry awards or recognitions Summarize the gathered evidence in a structured format. ~Step 4: Drafting the Unique Value Proposition Based on your analysis, draft a clear and compelling unique value proposition statement that encapsulates how [COMPANY NAME] differentiates itself in [INDUSTRY]. Ensure the statement: * Highlights core differentiators * Addresses both the emotional and practical benefits for the customer * Is concise and memorable Write a draft statement. ~Step 5: Review and Refinement Review the drafted value proposition with input from key stakeholders if possible. Reflect on whether the statement accurately reflects the company's strengths and market position. Make revisions as necessary for clarity and impact. ~Review/Refinement Ensure that the final unique value proposition meets the initial objectives outlined in Step 1 and is backed by the evidence and analysis provided in Steps 2 and 3. Consider if further market analysis is needed to solidify the statement. [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=297c3c2e-78d3-4f0e-bb9f-e7703469ccc1)

--- title: "Updates for Bob/Eleni" id: 14291 created: 2025-03-13T18:20:15Z modified: 2025-03-13T21:16:41Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "21e0c572-ed5c-4f9f-a694-b5bb3b9345fe" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: - url: "https://aka.ms/fabcon25/fbc?ocid=fabcon25_fbceb_blog_azdata" title: "March 31-April 2, 2025 at the MGM Grand in Las Vegas" - url: "mailto:pete@lif3away.com" title: "pete@lif3away.com" - url: "mailto:david@lif3away.com" title: "david@lif3away.com" - url: "mailto:callin@lif3away.com" title: "callin@lif3away.com" source: notes://showNote?identifier=d281c035-8044-4091-8707-25bb727222d4 --- Updates for Bob/Eleni **Quantexa Entity Resolution on MSFT Azure Fabric analytics** Mike Destein (head of partnerships and PM for ER SaaS) used to work for me and previously for Informatica. He has now built Quantexa’s new entity resolution solution on Azure Fabric analytics infrastructure. In contrast to Quantexa’s core AML/fraud prevention platform offerings (on-prem and cloud), this new solution is a cloud SaaS: pure-play product led growth. Two distinct opportunities emerged from today’s discussion ``` * Customer implementation ``` : Mike wants us to resell his solution, to which I politely declined and offered instead to provide staff augmentation to their PS team and Mike is okay with that approach—just needs credentials/references. ``` * In-house billing data warehouse ``` : Given their consumption-based metering model (1K records ~ $0.25), Quantexa needs to build a billing data warehouse and do NOT have the IT chops to get this done in-house. Very standard cloud marketplace integration work that is well-understood and readily defined. (And yes this was a great discovery call, as this second opportunity just came up today.). GA will be in August/October timeframe, so Mike expects there will be considerable urgency and soon. ``` Next steps - Mike will attend #FabCon Las Vegas for March 31-April 2, 2025 at the MGM Grand in Las Vegas - After the conference, there will be an internal budget discussion for both opportunities in early April ``` In the meanwhile, I will assemble a deck with our Azure analytics credentials and share that with Mike, along with an NDA Our next call will be primarily focused on scoping the data warehouse work **Lif3away Product Build** Lif3away is looking to disrupt rental markets, and my former colleague David is COO there. Once they have closed their upcoming round of funding, our product build work is expected to start in May. David asked for an NDA and MSA will be on our paper. I will get more technical details about architecture (Google Cloud for infrastructure, tech stack unknown) in my next call with their CIO/CTO, Callin. Fast-moving opportunity. Next steps NDA to legal ([pete@lif3away.com](mailto:pete@lif3away.com) and [david@lif3away.com](mailto:david@lif3away.com)) Introductory email to CITO ([callin@lif3away.com](mailto:callin@lif3away.com)) * Scoping call to follow **Amberdata** Chatted with Taylor about my connection to their CEO, Shawn Douglass. Valuable conversation for better insight into how best to work with CEs. Next steps - Monitor response from Shawn ``` - Engage Taylor based on his next steps ``` [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=d281c035-8044-4091-8707-25bb727222d4)

Jake knows nothing about BairesDev. * You're contacting Marta, Procurement Manager at J&J, who you met at a Health Tech Summit last week in New York. At the venue, she couldn't talk but gave you her business cards and asked you to reach out. * You're reconnecting with Paul, your 1st-degree contact with whom you went to college with, but hasn't spoken with in the past couple of years. I’m doing two quick messages back to back and including this as a single video to make it easy for you to view and I welcome your feedback here. First message is to Jake, CTO at AFLAC and then the second is to Marta, procurement at J&J. Here goes…:) Jake, this is Michael. Anne from your BD team is a former colleague—and it sounds like her move back to Ohio has gone great. Anne thinks that your technology teams would benefit from engaging with my firm, BairesDev, which is short for Buenos Aires Development. We offer nearshore custom software development services. All the economic benefits of outsourcing IN YOUR TIMEZONE. We can help AFLAC do more with less. I can work with your admin to get time on your calendar later this week and look forward to speaking with you then. Thanks, Jake! Marta, this is Michael. We met only briefly last week at the Health Tech Summit. You gave me your business card so thank you for that. I wanted to follow up with a video message to ask for time to dig in deeper on how we at BairesDev deliver greater software feature velocity with better economics and breakthrough business outcomes. We have some reference customers adjacent to J&J I would like to share under NDA. It would be great for us to schedule time together later this week. I look forward to speaking with you then. Thanks, Marta! Andrew, this is Michael. I wanted to drop a quick video message in your inbox as a way of saying thank you for the introduction to Alice the product manager [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=8fe14c59-08eb-4d5e-b3c7-ee0fa02fe3c8)

--- title: "Welcome message from Bob Leibholz" id: 14056 created: 2025-01-24T21:36:29Z modified: 2025-01-24T21:39:10Z folder: "BairesDev/Operations" attachments: - id: "d118a1e2-ce7e-4f87-b44b-7d33f1384cd2" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=9b817560-80b5-44a0-be24-d50dbc3114e7 --- Welcome message from Bob Leibholz Hey, it's Bob. I'm so glad you're joining BearsDev. It's a wonderful company and you're a terrific fit for this role. I think you're going to be spectacularly successful. I wanted to share some best practices, things that I've seen our top performers do in the early months that ensure their success, not just in the early months, for years down the road. There's a few things and none of this stuff is transcendentally complex. First thing, it's really important to frankly over-invest your efforts during months one, two, three. Why is that? Because the earlier you have a strong pipeline, the better life is because you will never have to worry about, oh my God, where is the next lead? It's much better to have the problem of how to be more selective or which ones to spend more time on than the problem of where that lead, next lead is coming from. It's always really important from a risk mitigation perspective. We're all senior sales professionals and we know that sometimes deals die. Companies get sold, the person you're speaking with can change jobs. Things happen and we can't control that. But what we can control is how we work with companies, how we work with people and how we build those relationships. But no less important, we can control the number of leads we have so that if a deal dies, it's a shame, but nothing more than a shame. You move on. To make sure that happens, there's a few key first week activities that we do and frankly that you'll do with us that ensure that success. The day you join officially, we'll do something called a network download with you. Then we'll take that wonderful information, run it through our systems, add a bunch of data, score your connections so you understand who's the most important person or people that you should be speaking with because they have the right title at the right company and that company is hiring, things like that. In addition to that or concurrent with that to be more accurate, you'll do something called a relationship mapping exercise. You go into our site and you literally click on a number one through six that describes the level of relationship you have with each contact. Try not to overthink it. The each transaction or each contact mapping will take you one to three seconds, maybe five seconds if you're clicking slowly, but you can go through these very, very quickly in a matter of a few hours. In addition to that, there's something called the low hanging fruit spreadsheet. The good news is you actually created about half of it already during the hiring process. I had asked you for a number of key people that you would be reaching out to, but to get a sense of where you were focusing and whether or not that was a good idea. And whether or not that was a good match for us. Obviously it was because you're here, but that also the exact same work you've already done becomes the cornerstone of your low hanging fruit list. So that in concert with the scored contacts that you've given us will help really identify and help you focus where one where you're spending your time. Never forget the honeymoon period that you have is two to three months for those first two to three months. People want to speak to. They want to understand why you changed jobs. They want to understand about the terrific company you change jobs for, and they're willing to speak to you. More importantly, they're willing to share their own stories of their last job and their new job and what that what they're doing and what might be interesting to you from a staffing or from a project perspective. You only have those first three months. So you need to strike why the iron is hot to support that you need to make sure that your LinkedIn is updated. That's a first week activity. And it's very important because you don't want to have the situation where somebody hears that you switch jobs, goes to your LinkedIn and see something different than what they expected. So that needs to be done in the early days. Obviously, you'll do your email signature in the next few days. That's pretty trivial. Kathleen will help you. Lastly, as far as important tips, don't spend a lot of time solving technology issues. We have really good people that will solve them for you. So if you have issues with hardware issues with your machine, you have access issues, you have software issues, don't spend hours trying to roto root them. Reach out to Kathleen, create a ticket or have IT solve your problem. That is a much better use of their time. And I'd rather you not get frustrated. As I said before, I'm thrilled that you joined. I am sure you're going to be spectacularly successful and have a great day. Thanks. Thank you. [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=9b817560-80b5-44a0-be24-d50dbc3114e7)

I don’t have a perfect answer, but here are the ingredients for us to work together if possible—here are my findings, proposal, and a potential idea. * Findings: BairesDev has delivery road miles on Azure Fabric, but not formal MSFT certification We have black belt-level engineers with delivery road miles on Azure Fabric analytics—and customer references as credentials. To be clear, before I got here BairesDev has grown MUCH faster than our peers by focusing on customer needs rather than going the more typical GSI path of partnering with platforms. That said, we are in the process of embarking on formalizing our hyperscaler partnerships with AWS/MSFT/GCP but this is still work in progress. Proposal: BairesDev as subcontractor/staff augmentation with Quantexa PS? The fastest, simplest way for us to help Quantexa customers get started on your new Azure Fabric solution would be to provide staff augmentation to Quantexa’s Professional Services team, potentially as subcontractor. If that works as a starting point, we can evolve our services partnership from there. Potential idea: BairesDev writes an app wrapper to accelerate customer on-boarding? Another way that we could add value to your new solution is by writing software around your solution that could help customers get moving faster and do more. Thanks, Michael [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=d11a7c23-bd4a-4012-bc47-6e41f03aa984)

--- title: "Quantexa call with Mike Destein" id: 14910 created: 2025-05-17T01:24:39Z modified: 2025-05-17T01:27:03Z folder: "BairesDev/Opportunities" attachments: - id: "e9a1e8f4-6976-4b9b-848d-6bd481755ff4" type: "public.jpeg" content: "e9a1e8f4-6976-4b9b-848d-6bd481755ff4.jpg" - id: "bac41510-395b-4506-b02c-6fd13bf197b5" type: "public.jpeg" content: "bac41510-395b-4506-b02c-6fd13bf197b5.jpg" - id: "b324fe52-1106-40a5-9400-29f8b65866ca" type: "public.jpeg" content: "b324fe52-1106-40a5-9400-29f8b65866ca.jpg" - id: "733b1aac-dfca-4c37-a949-efc210601f73" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#work" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=6ec60c63-425f-4d06-9fb3-1e616462a55d --- **Quantexa call with Mike Destein** Jeremy, Leonardo and Michael from BairesDev discussed a data warehouse and integration project with Quantexa, which involves building a solution to capture usage metrics, integrate with various platforms like Salesforce and Snowflake, and develop dashboards and reports. The key highlights were the proposed team structure, timeline, and pricing approach, as well as the need for BairesDev to demonstrate its expertise in data warehouse and marketplace integration to Quantexa's stakeholder, Ray O'Brien, before proceeding with the project. **Key points** * Quantexa is developing a product called Unify that leverages Microsoft Fabric's workload API and entity resolution capabilities. * Unify will have a consumption-based business model, requiring a data warehouse and billing application to track usage metrics and integrate with Azure Marketplace. * Quantexa needs help building the data warehouse, billing application, and integrations with Salesforce, Azure Marketplace, Snowflake, and NetSuite. * The project will also require BI reporting and analytics to track customer usage and growth. * Quantexa has internal data engineers but lacks data warehouse specialists and expertise with Microsoft metering APIs, so they are looking to BairesDev for those capabilities. * BairesDev proposes a staff augmentation engagement where they provide the missing technical resources while Quantexa owns the project governance and management. * The initial phase will be a design and scoping exercise to define the requirements and determine if a fixed-price or time-and-materials approach is better. * The design phase is estimated to take around 4 weeks, with the full project potentially taking 4-6 months to complete. * Quantexa wants to get the product generally available by September, but the billing/revenue capabilities may not be ready by then. * BairesDev will provide Quantexa with collateral on their data warehouse and marketplace integration expertise to help with the internal approval process. [#work](/tags/work) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=6ec60c63-425f-4d06-9fb3-1e616462a55d)

--- title: "1A vs. 2A in Alex’s life today" id: 14607 created: 2023-05-07T23:53:58Z modified: 2025-01-12T03:08:54Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "abcd7ef6-62a6-4b8b-b492-bf19f0ed600e" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=6518881f-a7fd-4b40-8b9d-9718d82dfb91 --- **1A vs. 2A in Alex’s life today** I was in the shower when the doorbell rang and came downstairs in a towel. I asked the police officer, “Am I going with you or are you coming in?” He responded: “Get dressed. I’m just here to talk.” Alex had texted a friend last night asking the question, “What if there is a mass shooter at our high school?” I think that’s a pretty reasonable question after yesterday’s mass shooting in Allen, Texas. Nonetheless, the friend’s parent called it in to the police and high school security. The police officer who came to our house was gracious and empathetic--well trained in mental health issues--his eyebrows went high when I said “ASD AND ADD...” Came to the pretty reasonable conclusion that Alex is not nor has ever been a threat. We will see what the system does next but the system is not built for AR-15s: 14,500 people killed by guns in 126 days * 200 mass shootings in 126 days 614 kids killed by guns in 126 days [#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=6518881f-a7fd-4b40-8b9d-9718d82dfb91)

url: "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/empty_name" title: "Empty Name" source: notes://showNote?identifier=ce4eea87-a54e-4a69-9818-d8b7cd55a182 --- **40 Useful Concepts You Should Know - by Gurwinder** [https://www.gurwinder.blog/p/40-useful-concepts-you-should-know](https://www.gurwinder.blog/p/40-useful-concepts-you-should-know) Baader-Meinhof Phenomenon: When we notice something new, like an unusual word, we start seeing it more often. It feels like it's become more common but really we're just more alert to it, and we confuse our attention with reality itself. Hence conspiracy theories. Ostrich Effect: We often try to avoid info that we fear will cause us stress. Thus bills and work emails remain unopened, bank balances remain unchecked. This is counterproductive because ignoring a problem doesn't eliminate the problem or your anxiety; it only prolongs them. Nobel Disease: We idolize those who excel in a particular field, inflating their egos and afflicting them with the hubris to opine on matters they know little about. By celebrating people for their intelligence, we make them stupid. Warnock's Dilemma: Online content that provokes people gets more engagement than content people merely agree with, which incentivizes content creators to be provocative. So much is alarming and enraging only because so much is trying to get your attention. Google Scholar Effect: We all get our answers from whatever tops the search results, so these results come to dominate a topic, muscling out unluckier viewpoints. Google has trapped us in an orgy of intellectual incest where everyone is drawing from the same tiny meme-pool. Paradox Of Unanimity: Researchers Gunn et al. (2016) found that when eyewitnesses unanimously agreed on the identity of suspects, they were more likely to be wrong. The more people agree, the less likely they are thinking for themselves. Therefore, beware of consensuses. Epistemic Humility: Instead of trying to be right, try to be less wrong. Avoiding idiocy is easier than achieving genius, and by beginning from the position that you don't know enough (which you don't), you'll gain more awareness of your blindspots and become harder to fool. Mimetic Desire: Craving is contagious; watching other people want a thing makes us want it too. It's why ads are so effective. But it puts us all into unnecessary competition as we fool ourselves into chasing what others are chasing simply because they are chasing it. Overblown Implications Effect “We think people judge us by a single success or failure, but they don’t. If you mess up one meal no one thinks you're a bad chef, and if you have one great idea no one thinks you're a genius. People just aren't thinking about you that much.” —Ethan Mollick Ellsberg Paradox: People prefer a clear risk over an unclear one, even if it's no safer. E.g. They'd rather bet on a ball picked from a mix of 50 red & 50 black balls than on one where the exact ratio of red to black balls is unknown. Helps explain market volatility. Veblen Goods: We often attach value to things simply because they're hard to get. People will be more attracted to a painting if it costs $3 million than if it costs $3. The price becomes a feature of the product in that it allows the buyer to signal affluence to others. Peter Principle: People in a hierarchy such as a business or government will be promoted until they suck at their jobs, at which point they will remain where they are. As a result, the world is filled with people who suck at their jobs. Meme Theory: An ideology parasitizes the mind, changing the host’s behavior so they spread it to other people. Therefore, a successful ideology (the only kind we hear about) is not configured to be true; it is configured only to be easily transmitted and easily believed. Gambler's Fallacy: We often feel we're owed luck for being unlucky. "The coin was heads 10 flips in a row, the next flip has gotta be tails!" But probability has no memory; it won't make amends for its past behavior. Therefore, treat every possibility independent of the past. Do Something Principle: We often procrastinate because we're intimidated by our task. So make your task less intimidating by dividing it into steps and focusing only on the next step. Action creates traction, so each step you take will facilitate the next. Lindy Effect: The longer a non-biological system has existed, the longer it's likely to exist, because its age demonstrates its ability to weather the fickleness of fashions and the erosion of eons. The Liar's Dividend: Teaching people about deepfakes and other disinfo doesn't make them skeptical of falsehoods as much as it makes them skeptical of reality. Amid such confusion, they default to believing what they want to, discounting anything they don't like as disinfo. Shibboleth: An absurd ideological belief is a form of tribal signaling. It signifies that one considers their ideology more important than truth, reason, or sanity. To one's allies, this is an oath of unwavering loyalty. To one's enemies, it is a threat display. The Potato Paradox: Alice has 100kg of potatoes, which are 99% water. She lets them dry till they are 98% water. What is their new weight? 50kg. Sound crazy? A reminder that the truth is often counterintuitive. Throat-Clearing: Before criticizing their own tribe, people feel the need to reaffirm their loyalty to the tribe. "I support X but..." They do this because other people cannot comprehend that someone could see flaws in anyone other than the enemy team. Law of Triviality: A company needs a nuclear reactor and a bike shed. Few workers understand reactors, but all understand sheds, so the shed becomes the focus of debate as everyone tries to enact their vision. Projects that require the least attention tend to get the most. Chilling Effect: When punishment for what people say becomes widespread, people stop saying what they really think and instead say whatever is needed to thrive in the social environment. Thus, limits on speech become limits on sincerity. Reiteration Effect: Joseph Goebbels said*, "Repeat a lie often enough and it becomes the truth," and he was right; repetition can make people believe things they otherwise wouldn't. *Goebbels didn't actually say this, but everyone thinks he did because of the Reiteration Effect. Naïve Realism: We know others are biased, but think we see the world as it is. Thus, teaching people about biases & fallacies doesn't make them doubt their own beliefs, it only makes them even more doubtful of their opponents'. Maybe posting this megathread was a mistake... Purity Spiral: Members of political tribes inevitably begin competing with their fellows to be the most ideologically pure. The constant one-upmanship toward moral superiority causes the whole group to gradually become more extreme. E.g. Maoist China, Twitter echo-chambers. Kayfabrication: Politics is pro-wrestling in suits. Opposing parties are collaborators in a greater system, whose choreographed conflict entertains and distracts us from what is really going on. Postjournalism: The press lost its monopoly on news when the internet democratized info. To save its business model, it pivoted from journalism into tribalism. The new role of the press is not to inform its readers but to confirm what they already believe. Curiosity Zone: Curiosity is the desire to fill gaps in knowledge. Thus, curiosity occurs not when you know nothing about something, but when you know a bit about it. So learn a little about as much as you can (like you're doing now!), and it will spur you to learn even more. Sorites Paradox: What's the minimum number of grains of sand needed to make a heap? We don't know, because human language (in this case the word "heap") is imprecise. If our language can't even quantify a heap, how can it resolve the complex questions we so fiercely debate? 30: Brandolini's Law (aka the Bullshit Asymmetry Principle): It takes a lot more energy to refute bullshit than to produce it. Hence, the world is full of unrefuted bullshit. * Algorithmic Blindspots : We find growth while searching for other things. Algorithms give us exactly what we want on demand, so we never need to search, and never find what we never knew we needed. If you wish to grow, defy the robots' recommendations. * Longtermism : a) Future people matter morally as much as people alive now. b) There are likely many more future people than people alive now. c) Small changes now can have huge repercussions in future. If these are true, should we be doing more for future generations? The Two-Minute Rule: If a task would take less than two minutes, do it immediately. This is because adding the task to your mental to-do list, keeping it in your memory, and managing the anxiety of not having done it will take far more effort than just doing it now. Promethean Gap: Technology is outpacing wisdom; we're changing the world faster than we can adapt to it. Lagging ever more behind accelerating progress, we're increasingly unable to foresee the effects of what we create. We're amassing the power of gods, yet we remain apes. Information-Action Ratio: The mark of useful info is that it makes us act differently. Most info we consume doesn't make us act differently; we just passively graze on it like cattle before defecating it undigested. Stop mindless scrolling and seek out info that changes you. Gurwinder's Third Paradox: In order for you to beat someone in a debate, your opponent needs to realize they've lost. Therefore, it's easier to win an argument against a genius than an idiot. Media Naturalness Theory: Writing has existed for <2% of human history, so our brains are not evolved for reading; we need vocal/facial cues for context. Thus, accept that you'll be misunderstood online, but never stop posting, for the only way to write clearer is by writing. Tilting At Windmills: An online stranger doesn't know you; all they have are a few vague impressions of you, too meager to form anything but a phantasm. So when they attack "you", they're really just attacking their own imagination, and there is no need to take it personally. Principle Of Humanity: Every single person is exactly what you would be if you were them. This includes your political opponents. So instead of dismissing them as evil or stupid, maybe seek to understand the circumstances that led them to their conclusions. Empty Name: We can be convinced that a concept is real by the mere fact that it has a name, but the world is full of names for things that aren't real (e.g. Batman). As such, assume nothing is true just because it has a name (including every concept in this megathread!) --- title: "ADHD Traits" id: 14690 created: 2023-05-17T17:00:37Z modified: 2024-09-06T17:09:45Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "ebc3e743-09e1-4e01-bfe7-95b15728cb4d" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: - url: "http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.00045776367188,-105.1214447021484&q=2245%20evening%20star%20ln" title: "2245 Evening Star Ln, Lafayette CO - United States" source: notes://showNote?identifier=785caf4d-1223-4d21-92e1-f1719777faf6 --- **ADHD Traits** Brendan Mahan ADHD & Neurodivergent Coach @adhdessentials 21h • 10 tweets • 2 min read A LOT of supposed ADHD traits aren’t actually ADHD traits. They’re really just traits that develop as a consequence of having the disorder.  Some of these traits are coping mechanisms, others are trauma responses. Here’s a few examples: Example 1:  "ADHD people are the life of the party!"  This is a coping mechanism.  A lot of ADHDers learn to embrace their impulsivity and hyperactivity to distract people from their struggles. *Closely related is the charismatic ADHDer.  A lot of us needed the benefit of the doubt to get past our mistakes in school and life.  Getting those breaks required figuring out how to talk to people with power over us.  It’s a skill that transfers to all areas of our lives. * Example 2:  "ADHDers are lazy.” It’s a trauma response.  If you’re asked all your life to do things just beyond your ability, and not given the right support, you’ll start to think your efforts aren’t enough, that you can’t do things well.  It’s easy to give up on trying. *Note:  No one is lazy.  Lazy doesn’t exist.  What you’re seeing is learned helplessness brought about by a consistent lack of skills, support, and/or resources.  Provide people with the skills/support/resources they need, and the “lazy” can be worked through. * Example 3:  "People with ADHD are inherently good problem solvers."  There’s two things happening here.  The first is a coping mechanism.  We tend to cause a lot of problems, so we get practiced at solving those problems and that helps us develop the skill. *The second is that the ADHD brain does tend to be better at divergent thinking (as opposed to convergent) - that is an ADHD trait.  And it aids in brainstorming new ideas around problems.  But convergent thinking is needed to whittle those ideas down and apply them. * Example 4:  "ADHDers have a heightened sense of justice."  Trauma response.  When you’ve gotten the short end of the stick most of your life, and had to struggle while others seemingly coasted, you tend to have a keener eye for injustice.  (Or you become jaded and uncaring.) *Now that you’ve got this lens, are there any other “ADHD traits” that you think fit into it?*   [2245 Evening Star Ln, Lafayette CO - United States](http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.00045776367188,-105.1214447021484&q=2245%20Evening%20Star%20Ln) [#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=785caf4d-1223-4d21-92e1-f1719777faf6)

--- title: "ADHD and burnout" id: 14693 created: 2024-04-29T16:44:54Z modified: 2024-09-04T00:56:48Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "0292327d-d3db-482e-abe2-8d8d0c158ca3" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: - url: "http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.00109481811523,-105.1211700439453&q=lafayette%20accident%20chiropractors" title: "Lafayette Accident Chiropractors, Lafayette CO - United States" source: notes://showNote?identifier=1c51bd94-7abc-4d26-b803-96490e35bd11 --- **ADHD and burnout** You’re not lazy. You’re exhausted AF. Maybe you’re even burnt-out. It’s no secret that ADHDers are more likely to be burnt-out than their neurotypical peers. This is because we often have to work twice as hard with executive dysfunction in a society not set up for our success. -Dr. Jen [Lafayette Accident Chiropractors, Lafayette CO - United States](http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.00109481811523,-105.1211700439453&q=Lafayette%20Accident%20Chiropractors) [#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=1c51bd94-7abc-4d26-b803-96490e35bd11)

--- title: "ADHD and shame" id: 14582 created: 2023-07-01T17:34:10Z modified: 2024-09-06T17:57:16Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "5c489676-7a65-40fa-805c-cec9c7afffca" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: - url: "http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.49570846557617,-106.8337707519531&q=1094%20uncochief%20cir" title: "1094 Uncochief Cir, Steamboat Springs CO - United States" source: notes://showNote?identifier=d1341d26-5f0f-44e7-9fd5-da9998f02a92 --- **ADHD and shame ** For those of us with ADHD, the shame of its impact on our lives can be overwhelming. It's like we inhabit a secret world, somehow managing to find a way to keep things going, despite the simplest tasks feeling gargantuan. Most people don't understand this. I don't blame them for not understanding it; I just think they would be shocked if they could get a glimpse into how we think and live. content: --- title: "Adults with ADHD face long-term social and economic challenges, study…" id: 15040 created: 2025-06-01T17:12:22Z modified: 2025-06-01T17:14:04Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "ac1e44e3-40e6-4ab8-b123-abff49c8e2d4" type: "org.webmproject.webp" content: "ac1e44e3-40e6-4ab8-b123-abff49c8e2d4.webp" - id: "9d7d9e02-6507-408a-81ee-2dffca1948f3" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: - url: "https://www.psypost.org/adults-with-adhd-face-long-term-social-and-economic-challenges-study-finds-even-with-medication/?__readwiselocation=" title: "psypost.org" - url: "https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/s0022395625000160" title: "Journal of Psychiatric Research" - url: "https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpsychires.2025.01.016" title: "Long-term effects of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) on social functioning and health care outcomes" source: notes://showNote?identifier=8843adbb-e445-46a7-8b0b-d93cc9d4cb77 --- ## Adults with ADHD face long-term social and economic challenges, study finds — even with medication A new study found that adults with ADHD face significant social and economic challenges, even when they consistently use medication. By age 30, they are less likely to be employed and more likely to rely on social support compared to their peers without ADHD. The research suggests that family background and early intervention may play a crucial role in improving outcomes for those with ADHD. [psypost.org](https://www.psypost.org/adults-with-adhd-face-long-term-social-and-economic-challenges-study-finds-even-with-medication/?__readwiseLocation=) Eric W. Dolan A new study published in the Journal of Psychiatric Research sheds light on the long-term challenges faced by individuals diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). The research shows that adults with ADHD are more likely to struggle with education, employment, and overall social functioning compared to those without the condition. Even when individuals adhered to prescribed medication over a 10-year period, their outcomes did not significantly improve in key areas such as educational attainment or employment by the age of 30. ADHD is a developmental condition that affects about 2–3% of children, with symptoms often continuing into adulthood. It is characterized by persistent patterns of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. These behaviors can make it difficult to succeed in academic, social, and workplace settings. While medication is commonly prescribed to manage symptoms, the long-term effects of treatment on real-world outcomes like education and employment have not been well studied. The researchers set out to understand how adults with ADHD are doing by age 30, and whether sticking to prescribed medication during young adulthood helps improve social outcomes. They also aimed to quantify the broader social and economic impact of ADHD, particularly in terms of healthcare costs and the presence of other mental health conditions. The research team used national data from Denmark’s health and social registries to track individuals diagnosed with ADHD between 1995 and 2016. To be included, patients had to be under 30 years old by 2005 or turn 30 by 2016, which allowed for at least a decade of follow-up data. In total, 4,897 people with ADHD were matched with 18,931 individuals from the general population. Matching was based on age, gender, and location. The researchers then compared the two groups across a range of outcomes, including education level, employment status, income, healthcare use, and psychiatric comorbidities. They also examined medication adherence by tracking how consistently ADHD patients filled prescriptions over the 10 years leading up to age 30. Adherence was defined as receiving at least 70% of the defined daily dose in a given year. The researchers then analyzed whether greater adherence led to better social and economic outcomes. Overall, adults with ADHD fared significantly worse than their peers in many areas of life by the time they reached 30. Compared to the control group, individuals with ADHD were more likely to live alone, less likely to have completed higher education, less likely to be employed, more likely to rely on social welfare programs, and more likely to have been diagnosed with additional psychiatric disorders. At age 30, only about 35% of adults with ADHD were employed, compared to over 74% in the control group. A striking 34% of those with ADHD were receiving social security benefits, and 12% were on disability pensions — much higher than their non-ADHD peers. The economic picture was also sobering. Adults with ADHD had lower predicted income from employment and significantly higher healthcare costs. On average, they received more than double the amount of public financial support compared to the control group. Mental health was another key difference. Psychiatric comorbidities were far more common among ADHD patients, including depression, anxiety, and substance use disorders. These additional conditions strongly influenced outcomes: people with more severe psychiatric comorbidities were much less likely to finish school or be employed. One of the central questions of the study was whether sticking to ADHD medication made a difference. Surprisingly, the answer was no — or at least not in the expected way. Adherence to medication between the ages of 21 and 30 did not increase the chances of completing a vocational or college-level education. Nor did it boost the odds of being employed at age 30. In fact, higher adherence to medication was modestly associated with lower odds of being employed, although the researchers believe this may be due to the fact that people with more severe symptoms — and thus worse outcomes — are also the ones who are more likely to stay on medication. Educational outcomes were most strongly predicted by whether the patient’s parents had higher levels of education. Similarly, parental education also influenced whether the individual was employed by age 30. This suggests that family background remains a powerful driver of success, even among those with access to medical treatment. The researchers acknowledged several limitations. One key issue is that the data do not capture the full range of non-medication treatments, such as therapy, special education services, or social support programs. These may play an important role in long-term success but were not included in the study. Another challenge is that the study design was observational, which means it cannot prove causation. For example, while medication adherence was not linked to better outcomes, this does not mean that medication is ineffective — it may be that the people who need it most already face serious disadvantages that medication alone cannot overcome. There was also limited information about the age at which patients were first diagnosed. Many participants received their diagnosis as adults, missing out on early intervention that might have changed their life trajectories. The researchers note that earlier diagnosis and a combination of treatments might lead to better long-term results, but more studies are needed to test this idea. The study, “Long-term effects of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) on social functioning and health care outcomes,” was authored by Poul Jennum, Anne Virring Sørensen, Lone Baandrup, Michael Ibsen, Rikke Ibsen, and Jakob Kjellberg. [#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=8843adbb-e445-46a7-8b0b-d93cc9d4cb77)
