--- title: "Alex Essays x 2 (September 17, 1983)" id: 14642 created: 2024-10-14T00:11:33Z modified: 2025-01-04T04:09:04Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "d1a15ce0-a37b-40b5-9ffe-a23d15927a77" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=5863cca3-6772-4fef-94b5-00136c7319b2 --- **Alex Essays x 2 (September 17, 1983)** Non-verbal communications Both my parents communicate extensively in non-verbal ways, especially with their hands, but with very different intents and VERY different outcomes. My father communicates non-verbally using his hands and body as if he were conducting a symphony of ideas, trying to bring explainable order to concepts as if he were their conductor. Listening to him seek to ensure that I can follow along with his explanation of concepts almost like a mime performance seeks to tell a story without words—but he keeps talking all the while. * He asks lots of questions and works hard using non-verbal queues to engage and persuade his conversational counterpart of his intellectual goals. He has been trained in how to present well at work (software sales) and that shows up when he talks with me.⠀ * My mother also communicates non-verbally expressing her emotions—usually some variation of frustration—with her hands. Five years ago my mother suffered from a traumatic brain injury and that impacted her cognitive functions, especially emotional language and short-term memory, that much harder. I’m proud of her for coming such a long way since then, but her non-verbal communications still lag her actual verbal abilities. She mostly expresses frustration with her hands and bodily gestures.⠀ When my parents communicate with each other while being observed, they are much more restrained in their non-verbal communications individually and together. It’s harder to keep track of what each person is saying with fewer non-verbal queues, but we are lucky to be such a close family working on helping each other and overcoming all the challenges modern post-pandemic life present to us. I love my family. ⠀[#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=5863cca3-6772-4fef-94b5-00136c7319b2)

--- title: "Alex Shaler's 2016 Science Project" id: 14696 created: 2024-10-14T00:11:33Z modified: 2024-10-15T00:46:20Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "8e125de3-e05e-462f-9e0b-b8556f3f329b" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: [] source: notes://showNote?identifier=948d3d47-c4cf-437e-88cc-529ac4a34184 --- **Alex Shaler's 2016 Science Project** **Scientific Method Form** **Question:** Can falling water alone create electricity?⠀ How does water compare to wind as a source of energy? ⠀ **Hypothesis** I think that with enough pressure falling water can create electricity. **Method** Adapt a small *windmill* to become a *watermill* generator that translates motion over spinning magnets into electricity. **Results** When I blew on the small windmill, the light lit up. When I let water flow only using gravity over the waterwheel, the light did not light up.⠀ When I applied pressure by squeezing water out of a bottle, the light DID light up. ⠀ **Conclusion** If the wheel spins fast enough using wind or water pressure, the small generator can generate enough electricity to light up the light bulb. Gravity alone does not generate enough water power for electricity. With small motors, wind is better than water for making electricity. ⠀ **Materials** 2 small windmills with generator motors and gears⠀ Wiring Pipecleaners⠀ Super glue⠀ Wood structure and spray paint⠀ Balsa wood for watermill wheel⠀ Water bottle⠀ Plastic bin⠀ Tools (drill and screwdriver) [#Alex](/tags/Alex) ⠀ --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=948d3d47-c4cf-437e-88cc-529ac4a34184)

content: --- title: "Alex and Shame: What I Want You to Understand" id: 14711 created: 2021-11-28T20:59:52Z modified: 2024-09-06T00:34:36Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "961b7268-88e9-4b26-a66f-5e2522f6bc18" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: - url: "https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=29358993" title: "https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=29358993" - url: "http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.00015258789062,-105.1210632324219&q=9894%e2%80%939944%20w%20baseline%20rd" title: "9894–9944 W Baseline Rd, Lafayette CO - United States" source: notes://showNote?identifier=699dbe5b-c833-4429-9976-3e03f149f470 --- **Alex and Shame: What I Want You to Understand** Via HN: [https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=29358993](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=29358993) *“As an aspie myself, quite a few of these are accurate, but a number of them are just plain apologism. For example, Empathy (and pragmatism!) allows face-saving, but from an informational point-of-view, it contaminates the database and aspies are more dedicated to keeping the record clear and accurate. If you are an aspie or are good friends with one, you know this is in no way a dedication to objectivity, because it ends at our own skin. We are not good at accepting criticism, especially valid and accurate criticism. We feel humiliation very, oftentimes unreasonably, strongly. Being different from most people does not mean all our differences are disabilities, but at the same time it also doesn't mean none of them are. Stop romanticizing it and recognize that explanations are not excuses for being an asshole. We are not tragically misunderstood ubermensch and the world would not be a better place if everyone was like us.”* [9894–9944 W Baseline Rd, Lafayette CO - United States](http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.00015258789062,-105.1210632324219&q=9894%E2%80%939944%20W%20Baseline%20Rd) [#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=699dbe5b-c833-4429-9976-3e03f149f470)

--- title: "Alex’s ADD realization tonight" id: 14708 created: 2024-04-07T03:00:42Z modified: 2024-09-04T00:57:10Z folder: "Family/Alex" attachments: - id: "89b08245-00bd-4ad3-97c7-1e5ccf0436b3" type: "com.apple.notes.inlinetextattachment.hashtag" content: "#Alex" links: - url: "http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.000732421875,-105.1213302612305&q=2245%20evening%20star%20ln" title: "2245 Evening Star Ln, Lafayette CO - United States" source: notes://showNote?identifier=7beb23ec-8712-45b5-a09c-0e40fdaa1d62 --- **Alex’s ADD realization tonight** Alex: “Dad, why does this keep happening?” Me: “Why does what keep happening?” Alex: “Why do I keep wasting time?” Me: “What do you mean?” Alex: “If I could have gotten my homework done earlier, I could have watched the NCAA Final Four games at my friends’ house.” Me: “Look at it this way: you were SUPER FOCUSED when you were working toward that very tangible time-bound goal. That is awesome and you should be proud of what you did there.” Alex: “Thanks Dad.” [2245 Evening Star Ln, Lafayette CO - United States](http://maps.apple.com/?ll=40.000732421875,-105.1213302612305&q=2245%20Evening%20Star%20Ln) [#Alex](/tags/Alex) --- [Open in Notes](notes://showNote?identifier=7beb23ec-8712-45b5-a09c-0e40fdaa1d62)
